prompt: A facility manager wants a quick screening of energy cost drivers before committing to detailed analysis. Which audit level is most appropriate?

options.text: ASHRAE Level I walk-through with utility review and low-cost/no-cost opportunities
ASHRAE Level III investment-grade audit with submetering and risk allocation
Retro-commissioning functional test of every air-handling unit
Full life-cycle cost analysis of a new chiller plant only

explanation: Level I audits provide a walk-through assessment, utility data review, and identification of obvious ECMs suitable for initial screening without the cost and rigor of investment-grade analysis.

distractorExplanations.b: Level III is reserved for high-stakes financing where savings must be verified with greater precision.
distractorExplanations.c: Retro-commissioning focuses on system performance verification, not initial screening of cost drivers.
distractorExplanations.d: A single-equipment LCC study does not substitute for a whole-facility screening audit.

prompt: An energy manager compares two ECMs using simple payback. Project A saves $12,000/year with a $36,000 cost. Project B saves $8,000/year with a $20,000 cost. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Project A has the shorter simple payback period
Project B has the shorter simple payback period
Both projects have identical payback periods
Simple payback cannot be used for energy projects

explanation: Simple payback equals initial cost divided by annual savings. Project A: 3.0 years; Project B: 2.5 years, so Project B returns capital faster on a simple payback basis.

distractorExplanations.a: Project A's payback is 3.0 years, which is longer than Project B's 2.5 years.
distractorExplanations.c: The cost-to-savings ratios differ, producing different payback periods.
distractorExplanations.d: Simple payback is commonly used for preliminary screening, though it ignores time value of money.

prompt: Under a time-of-use (TOU) electric rate, which operating strategy most directly reduces energy charges?

options.text: Raising thermostat setpoints during off-peak hours only
Running base-load equipment only during on-peak periods
Shifting discretionary loads to off-peak periods when energy prices are lower
Increasing power factor without changing load schedule

explanation: TOU rates charge different energy prices by time period. Moving flexible loads to off-peak hours lowers the energy ($/kWh) portion of the bill.

distractorExplanations.b: Operating during on-peak periods increases exposure to the highest energy rates.
distractorExplanations.d: Power factor correction affects demand/reactive charges, not TOU energy pricing by itself.
distractorExplanations.a: Thermostat changes affect consumption but do not inherently align loads with lower-priced periods.

prompt: A corporate energy policy sets a target to reduce energy use intensity (EUI) by 20% from a baseline year. Which metric best tracks progress?

options.text: Peak demand in kW only
Total annual energy consumption in kWh regardless of floor area
Energy cost per employee headcount
Annual site energy divided by conditioned floor area

explanation: EUI normalizes total site energy use by floor area (commonly kBtu/ft²·yr or kWh/m²·yr), enabling fair comparison across years and portfolio buildings.

distractorExplanations.a: Peak demand reflects load magnitude, not normalized energy intensity.
distractorExplanations.b: Absolute consumption ignores changes in building size or occupancy normalization.
distractorExplanations.c: Cost per employee mixes rate effects with usage and is not standard EUI.

prompt: Which element is typically required in an ASHRAE Level II energy audit but not in a Level I audit?

options.text: Detailed energy-use breakdown and savings calculations with economic analysis for recommended ECMs
A brief walk-through of the facility
Review of utility bills
Identification of obvious low-cost measures

explanation: Level II expands on Level I with more detailed survey data, end-use breakdowns, quantified savings, and economic evaluation of recommended measures.

distractorExplanations.b: Walk-throughs are part of Level I screening.
distractorExplanations.c: Utility bill review is common to both Level I and Level II.
distractorExplanations.d: Low-cost measure identification is a Level I deliverable.

prompt: A utility tariff includes a demand ratchet clause set at 80% of the highest monthly peak in the prior 12 months. What is the practical effect?

options.text: Demand charges are eliminated in summer months
The customer may pay demand charges based on a floor even if actual peak is lower
Energy charges are replaced by demand charges
Power factor penalties are waived automatically

explanation: Ratchet clauses set a minimum billing demand based on historical peaks, so a single high peak can elevate demand charges for months even when current peaks are lower.

distractorExplanations.a: Ratchets typically apply year-round or in defined seasons, not as a summer elimination.
distractorExplanations.c: Energy and demand charges remain separate bill components.
distractorExplanations.d: Power factor rules are independent of demand ratchet provisions.

prompt: When evaluating an ECM with a 15-year life, which economic method accounts for the time value of money and all cash flows over the project life?

options.text: Simple payback period only
First-year savings divided by installed cost
Net present value (NPV) using a discount rate
Gross floor area per dollar of capital cost

explanation: NPV discounts future savings and costs to present value, capturing the time value of money across the full project lifecycle.

distractorExplanations.a: Simple payback ignores cash flows after payback and discounting.
distractorExplanations.b: That ratio is a simplified metric, not a full lifecycle evaluation.
distractorExplanations.d: Floor area normalization is unrelated to discounted cash flow analysis.

prompt: IPMVP Option A is most appropriate when:

options.text: Whole-facility savings must be determined using utility bill regression only
No baseline period is available
Savings are verified solely by engineering estimates without field data
Individual equipment retrofit savings are isolated with periodic short-term measurements

explanation: IPMVP Option A (retrofit isolation) uses short-term measurements of key parameters combined with stipulated values for other factors to quantify savings for specific retrofits.

distractorExplanations.a: Whole-facility utility analysis aligns more with Option C or D approaches.
distractorExplanations.b: All IPMVP options require a defined baseline period.
distractorExplanations.c: Option A requires measured data for critical parameters, not estimates alone.

prompt: A state adopts the latest commercial building energy code based on ASHRAE Standard 90.1. For new construction, the code primarily establishes:

options.text: Mandatory minimum efficiency requirements for building systems and envelope
Voluntary targets that owners may ignore without penalty
Retrofit requirements for all existing buildings immediately
Utility rate structures for commercial customers

explanation: Model energy codes like IECC and ASHRAE 90.1 set enforceable minimum efficiency requirements for new construction and major renovations.

distractorExplanations.b: Energy codes are legally enforceable minimum standards, not optional guidance.
distractorExplanations.c: Existing building retrofits are typically addressed by separate policies, not new-construction code alone.
distractorExplanations.d: Rate design is a utility regulatory function, not a building energy code.

prompt: Life-cycle cost (LCC) analysis for a boiler replacement should include which cost category that simple payback typically omits?

options.text: Initial capital cost only
Ongoing maintenance, fuel, and replacement costs over the analysis period
The color of the boiler casing
First-year energy savings only

explanation: LCC sums all relevant costs—capital, energy, maintenance, and periodic replacements—over the analysis horizon, often in present-value terms.

distractorExplanations.d: LCC considers multiple years of operating costs, not first-year savings alone.
distractorExplanations.a: Capital cost alone ignores operating and maintenance impacts.
distractorExplanations.c: Cosmetic attributes are not part of economic LCC analysis.

prompt: An energy manager normalizes monthly electricity use against heating degree days (HDD) and cooling degree days (CDD). The primary purpose is to:

options.text: Determine the utility demand ratchet automatically
Eliminate the need for submetering
Adjust consumption for weather variation when tracking performance
Calculate power factor penalties

explanation: Weather normalization adjusts energy use for HDD/CDD so performance comparisons reflect operational changes rather than weather-driven swings.

distractorExplanations.b: Normalization complements but does not replace end-use submetering.
distractorExplanations.d: Power factor is calculated from kW and kVAR, not degree-day data.
distractorExplanations.a: Ratchets are tariff provisions, not derived from degree-day normalization.

prompt: Which scenario best justifies an ASHRAE Level III investment-grade audit?

options.text: Replacing a single LED lamp in a storage closet
Reviewing last month's utility bill for anomalies only
A quick staff training walk-through of a small office
An ESPC or performance contract where savings guarantee repayment of third-party financing

explanation: Level III audits provide the rigor, measurement, and risk analysis needed when savings estimates underpin financing guarantees in ESPCs or similar arrangements.

distractorExplanations.c: Small offices rarely need investment-grade audit depth for screening.
distractorExplanations.a: Minor measures do not warrant Level III cost and analysis.
distractorExplanations.b: Bill review alone is far below Level III scope.

prompt: A facility's electric bill shows 500 kW peak demand billed at $12/kW and 200,000 kWh at $0.08/kWh. What is the approximate total demand plus energy charge?
explanation: Demand charge: 500 kW × $12/kW = $6,000. Energy charge: 200,000 kWh × $0.08/kWh = $16,000. Combined total = $22,000.

distractorExplanations.d: This reflects demand charges only, omitting energy charges.
distractorExplanations.b: This reflects energy charges only, omitting demand charges.
distractorExplanations.c: This overstates the bill by adding incorrect components.

prompt: A municipal energy policy requires benchmarking public buildings using ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager. The normalized score primarily compares:

options.text: Annual water use to parking stall count
Actual source EUI to the national median for similar building types
Indoor CO₂ levels to ASHRAE 62.1 limits
Chiller tonnage to roof area only

explanation: Portfolio Manager scores compare a building's weather-normalized source EUI against peer buildings of the same type to produce a 1–100 performance score.

distractorExplanations.c: Indoor air quality metrics are not the basis of ENERGY STAR building scores.
distractorExplanations.d: Equipment sizing ratios are not the normalization basis for benchmarking scores.
distractorExplanations.a: Water use and parking counts are unrelated to the energy score algorithm.

prompt: An ECM has a positive NPV at a 6% discount rate but a simple payback of 9 years. Which conclusion is most accurate?

options.text: Positive NPV means payback must be under 2 years
Simple payback is always superior to NPV for long-life projects
The project creates value on a discounted basis despite a long simple payback
NPV and payback always agree, so the analysis must be wrong

explanation: NPV can be positive when long-term savings outweigh upfront cost even if simple payback is lengthy, because NPV accounts for the full savings stream and discount rate.

distractorExplanations.d: Payback and NPV measure different aspects and can diverge, especially with long-lived savings.
distractorExplanations.a: There is no fixed payback threshold tied to NPV positivity.
distractorExplanations.b: For long-life projects, NPV and LCC typically provide more complete economic insight than payback alone.

prompt: Which federal policy mechanism most directly encourages energy efficiency in federal buildings through required energy reduction targets?

options.text: Coast Guard navigation lighting standards
National park visitor fee schedules
Corporate average fuel economy standards for passenger vehicles
Federal energy management requirements such as EISA/ECPA energy intensity reduction goals

explanation: Federal statutes and executive orders establish energy intensity reduction targets and reporting for federal agencies, driving efficiency in government facilities.

distractorExplanations.c: CAFE standards regulate vehicle fuel economy, not federal building operations.
distractorExplanations.a: Navigation lighting standards are unrelated to federal building energy policy.
distractorExplanations.b: Park fee schedules do not establish federal facility energy reduction mandates.

prompt: During M&V planning, the baseline period should:

options.text: Represent pre-retrofit conditions with sufficient data to characterize normal operation
Begin only after all ECMs are installed
Exclude all weather data
Use estimated savings instead of measured consumption

explanation: A defensible baseline captures pre-retrofit performance over an adequate period so post-retrofit consumption can be compared against adjusted baseline conditions.

distractorExplanations.b: The baseline must precede retrofit implementation to establish pre-project performance.
distractorExplanations.c: Weather adjustment is often required for fair baseline comparisons.
distractorExplanations.d: M&V relies on measured or modeled consumption, not savings estimates alone for verification.

prompt: A commercial customer on a real-time pricing tariff faces high prices during a grid emergency. The best demand-response strategy is to:

options.text: Increase production loads to maximize throughput
Reduce or shift non-critical loads during the high-price event
Disable all metering to avoid billing
Run all HVAC at minimum outdoor air regardless of price

explanation: Real-time pricing exposes customers to hourly market signals; curtailing discretionary loads during emergencies lowers cost and supports grid reliability.

distractorExplanations.a: Increasing load during price spikes raises cost and grid stress.
distractorExplanations.c: Metering cannot be disabled; consumption is still billed.
distractorExplanations.d: Minimum ventilation settings do not inherently respond to price signals.

prompt: Internal rate of return (IRR) for an energy project is best described as:

options.text: Annual kWh savings divided by peak kW
The utility's published demand charge
The discount rate at which NPV equals zero
The simple payback multiplied by the discount rate

explanation: IRR is the discount rate that makes the present value of inflows equal outflows (NPV = 0), often compared to a hurdle rate for project approval.

distractorExplanations.d: IRR is not derived from simple payback multiplied by a discount rate.
distractorExplanations.a: kWh/kW is a load factor-related ratio, not IRR.
distractorExplanations.b: Demand charges are tariff rates, not project return metrics.

prompt: An energy audit report lists a measure with 35% savings uncertainty at 90% confidence. This indicates:

options.text: The measure will definitely save exactly 35%
No economic evaluation was performed
The measure is exempt from M&V
The savings estimate includes quantified uncertainty bounds from the analysis

explanation: Investment-grade and detailed audits report uncertainty ranges so decision-makers understand savings risk; ±35% at 90% confidence bounds the expected outcome.

distractorExplanations.a: Uncertainty bands mean the exact savings are not guaranteed at a single value.
distractorExplanations.b: Uncertainty reporting can accompany full economic evaluation.
distractorExplanations.c: M&V requirements depend on contract structure, not the presence of uncertainty bands.

prompt: Which data source is most essential for developing an energy balance during a Level II audit?

options.text: 12 months of utility bills plus end-use estimates from survey and spot measurements
Employee satisfaction survey results only
Architectural renderings without as-built data
Social media reviews of the building

explanation: Energy balances reconcile total utility consumption with end uses using billing data, equipment inventories, and targeted measurements from the Level II survey.

distractorExplanations.b: Occupant satisfaction does not quantify end-use energy distribution.
distractorExplanations.c: Renderings lack operational and as-built performance data needed for balances.
distractorExplanations.d: Public reviews provide no technical energy data.

prompt: A plant receives a utility rebate for installing high-efficiency equipment. In LCC analysis, the rebate should be treated as:

options.text: A penalty increasing simple payback
A reduction in initial capital cost (or equivalent incentive cash flow)
An increase in annual maintenance cost
Excluded entirely from all economic analysis

explanation: Incentives and rebates reduce net capital investment, improving payback, NPV, and IRR and should be included in the project's cash flow analysis.

distractorExplanations.c: Rebates do not increase maintenance costs.
distractorExplanations.d: Ignoring incentives overstates net cost and understates project viability.
distractorExplanations.a: Rebates shorten rather than lengthen payback.

prompt: IPMVP Option C (whole facility) is commonly selected when:

options.text: No utility meter serves the facility
Baseline and reporting periods must be identical calendar months only with no adjustments
Multiple interactive ECMs affect the entire meter and individual isolation is impractical
Only a single lamp is replaced

explanation: Option C uses whole-facility utility data with a calibrated model to verify savings when multiple measures interact and retrofit isolation is not feasible.

distractorExplanations.d: Single small measures are better suited to Option A or B isolation approaches.
distractorExplanations.a: Option C requires facility-level meter data.
distractorExplanations.b: Option C allows routine adjustments for weather, occupancy, and other independent variables.

prompt: A facility energy policy mandates purchase of ENERGY STAR certified office equipment. This policy lever primarily reduces:

options.text: Structural wind loads on the envelope
City property tax assessments automatically
Natural gas combustion in the boiler room only
Plug load and office equipment energy consumption

explanation: ENERGY STAR equipment standards target efficient plug loads for computers, monitors, copiers, and similar devices covered by the program.

distractorExplanations.a: Equipment procurement policies do not address structural wind loading.
distractorExplanations.b: Tax assessments are unrelated to equipment efficiency procurement rules.
distractorExplanations.c: Office equipment policies do not directly affect boiler fuel use.

prompt: An auditor finds that production increased 20% after a lighting retrofit while energy use fell 10%. For fair savings reporting, the energy manager should:

options.text: Adjust the baseline for production or other relevant independent variables in the M&V plan
Ignore the retrofit because production changed
Use only the post-retrofit month as the new baseline
Report 10% savings with no adjustments

explanation: M&V protocols require routine adjustments for variables like production, occupancy, or weather so reported savings reflect the ECM, not unrelated activity changes.

distractorExplanations.d: Unadjusted comparisons confound production-driven energy changes with retrofit effects.
distractorExplanations.b: Production changes are handled through adjustment, not by discarding the project.
distractorExplanations.c: The pre-retrofit baseline remains the reference; post data alone cannot redefine baseline.
